coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of pH-sensitive chitosan microspheres for delivery of curcumin; characterization, drug release kinetics and evaluation of anticancer activity
coredata.dc:creator: Pooresmaeil, Malihe
Namazi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: Curcumin (CUR) is a lowly water-soluble natural polyphenol with chemopreventive and chemotherapeutic activities. Hence, to achieve the system with good CUR loading ability, porous MIL-88 (Fe) was prepared in the presence of the presynthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (GQDs@MIL-88 (Fe)). In the following, CUR loaded in the fabricated GQDs@MIL-88 (Fe) nanohybrid. The characterization techniques; Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscope (SEM), photoluminescence (PL), and Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis showed success in the synthesis of GQDs@MIL-88 (Fe). Moreover, the FT-IR analysis displayed the loading of CUR and the formation of CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). Chitosan (CS) was used as a green coating to enhance the biocompatibility of the prepared system (CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). The fabricated microspheres showed pH-sensitive swelling behavior and released 38.3% of CUR in pH 5.0 which is better fitted with the First-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.9726). In comparison with CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), the MTT and DAPI assay exhibited less toxic effect for CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) against MDA-MB 231 cells. Moreover, the safety of the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) confirmed after incubation against MCF 10A as a model of the normal cell line. The results conveyed a new concept that the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) is a potential candidate for using as a biocompatible carrier with controlled drug delivery ability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Graphene quantum dots
MIL-88(Fe)
Curcumin
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MIL-88(Fe) in the presence of the GQDs
Loading of CUR in GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) nanohybrid
Facile coating of the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) with CS
Study the swelling of CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) microspheres
Study of the CUR release kinetic
In vitro drug load and delivery studies
Evaluation of the CUR release kinetics from the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (Grant Number 948645103) and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, the Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for their financial support of this research.

Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (Grant Number 948645103 ) and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, the Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for their financial support of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of GQDs, GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), CUR/GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), CS, and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe).
EDX spectrum for the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (a) and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (b) with the corresponding elemental mapping of CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (c).
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm and BJH plot of GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (a,b), and zeta potential analysis of GQDs, GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (c).
XRD (a), and PL spectra (b) of the GQDs, GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe).
SEM images of GQDs (a), GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) In two different magnifications (b,c), and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (d).
Results of the carried out swelling for CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) at 37 °C (a), drug loading values for GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (b).
CUR release behavior of CS/CUR (a), CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (b) at two different pH, and the schematic of CUR release from CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (c).
The MTT assay results after 24 h incubation of the samples at 37 °C against the MDA-MB 231 cells (a) and MCF 10A (b). Results are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3).
Fluorescence microscopy images of incubated MDA-MB 231 cells with CUR (b), CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (c) and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (d) for 24 h. Untreated cells assigned as control (a).
The schematic illustration of the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) preparation, CUR loading, and encapsulation of CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) with CS biopolymer.
Kinetic results of the experimental CUR release fitting with kinetic equations.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of pH-sensitive chitosan microspheres for delivery of curcumin; characterization, drug release kinetics and evaluation of anticancer activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nano-Biopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran. Tel.: +98 4133393121, Fax: +98 4133340191,

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Curcumin (CUR) is a lowly water-soluble natural polyphenol with chemopreventive and chemotherapeutic activities. Hence, to achieve the system with good CUR loading ability, porous MIL-88 (Fe) was prepared in the presence of the presynthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (GQDs@MIL-88 (Fe)). In the following, CUR loaded in the fabricated GQDs@MIL-88 (Fe) nanohybrid. The characterization techniques; Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscope (SEM), photoluminescence (PL), and Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis showed success in the synthesis of GQDs@MIL-88 (Fe). Moreover, the FT-IR analysis displayed the loading of CUR and the formation of CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). Chitosan (CS) was used as a green coating to enhance the biocompatibility of the prepared system (CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). The fabricated microspheres showed pH-sensitive swelling behavior and released 38.3% of CUR in pH 5.0 which is better fitted with the First-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.9726). In comparison with CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), the MTT and DAPI assay exhibited less toxic effect for CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) against MDA-MB 231 cells. Moreover, the safety of the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) confirmed after incubation against MCF 10A as a model of the normal cell line. The results conveyed a new concept that the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) is a potential candidate for using as a biocompatible carrier with controlled drug delivery ability.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite the comprehensive information about cancer biology, in the past decades, the rate of cancer death is increasing. Therefore, today's cancer is one of the serious illnesses that lead to the death of the million people in the worldwide. Chemotherapy is the commonly used technique for cancer treatment; however, it has several drawbacks that side effects on the normal cells and low therapeutic efficiency in the treatment process are two main examples of these drawbacks [1–4]. Drug delivery systems based on nanoparticles (NPs) can overcome these problems to obtain a new system with more efficiency [5–12]. Controlled drug release, targeted drug delivery, and protection of the drugs from the degradation are some advantages of the drug delivery systems based on the NPs [13–16]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), GQDs, magnetite, graphene oxide (GO), and layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are some examples of the favorite NPs for use in drug delivery area [17–21].

Fluorescent GQDs are a new member of the nanocarbon family which attracted the growing attention due to their special features; conjugated π electron systems, abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, physicochemical stability, strong fluorescence intensity, and good biocompatibility. These valuable properties make the GQDs as an appropriate candidate for use in bio-imaging, cancer therapy, diagnostic analysis, drug delivery, and fluorescent labeling [22–25]. GQDs could be combined with other NPs to form the new nanohybrid with upgraded features. MOFs are a new class of the crystalline porous materials which their synthesis in the presence of the GQDs formed the photoluminescent and porous nanohybrid. The tunable pore sizes, low density, and the high surface area provide their possible use in various areas such as drug delivery [22,26–30]. Therefore, to acquire the new nanovehicle with features of both MOFs and GQDs, we developed the GQDs@MOFs as a platform for the CUR loading.

CUR is the low molecular weight natural polyphenol with the lower solubility in the water, low price, and non-toxicity against normal cells even at higher doses (12 g/day in humans). CUR is extracted from the rhizome of turmeric and extensively used in a wide range of medicinal application areas [13,31]. This drug has the potential of apoptosis-inducing in various types of cancer cells like breast, colon, lung, and prostate. In the treatment of breast cancer, CUR prevents the expression of proliferating cell nuclear antigen, cyclin D1, β-catenin, and p53 mRNAs. However, it has several drawbacks like low solubility in water, instability in UV irradiation, and alkaline environments that limit its usage in medicine [32–34]. Several attempts tried to overcome these limitations via different encapsulation techniques that microcapsules, complexes, micelles, and liposomes are some of them. The encapsulation technique could protect the drug from the environmental conditions and improves its half-life which leads to enhanced bioavailability [35]. Moreover, the nano-based drug carriers could protect the encapsulated drugs from the degradation and afford the controlled and sustained drug release, and therefore therapeutic index of the loaded drug improved. In fact, the nanocarriers create CUR readily soluble in aqueous solution and develop its bioavailability to cancer cells [36–38]. Because the new considerations have been focused on the preparation of the drug delivery systems via using the materials that are biocompatible and safe, therefore, in this work CS as a safe biological macromolecule with inherent antibacterial activity was used as a green coat in the structure of the prepared carrier to encapsulate the CUR loaded GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) nanohybrid.

Carbohydrates are biocompatible materials that could be used as safe carriers for drug delivery applications. Among the carbohydrates, CS is the second abundant antibacterial and renewable natural polysaccharide with a linear structure. CS is prepared from the partial deacetylation of chitin [39–44]. CS is polycationic in nature (pKa = 6.5) and it is composed of 4-linked-β-2-amino-2-deoxy-glucopyranose units that some of them are N-acetylated [45–47]. Bio-degradability, controlled release, non-toxicity, and bio-compatibility of CS have led to enhanced focus for use of its in biomedical researches [48]. The preparation of the CS network via its crosslinking is the commonly used technique that able to improve some of the neat CS properties. Crosslinking could occur through physical or chemical methods. In comparison two these methods, chemical crosslinking is the better way to reach the system with enhanced strength. Glutaraldehyde, formaldehyde, and genipin are some of the commonly used chemical crosslinkers which could crosslink the CS biopolymer [46,49–52]. The coating of the drug-loaded nanohybrids with biocompatible and pH-sensitive CS as microspheres through the crosslinking could keep the drug from degradation and enhance its biocompatibility. In recent years, excessive research has been devoted to study the CS-based systems as drug delivery vehicles. However, the search on the so far published papers to this day display that there is not any published work for evaluation of the CS coated CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) as a bio-vehicle for controlled delivery of CUR.

Hence, with the aim of the preparation new drug delivery system with suitable CUR loading ability and good biocompatibility, herein, we reported an easy method for the synthesis of the porous and photoluminescent GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). The prepared GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) nanohybrid was characterized by using several techniques. CUR as a hydrophobic drug was loaded in the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). To improve the biocompatibility of the system, CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was encapsulated by using the CS biopolymer as a pH-sensitive microsphere. The drug release behavior of the final system was explored at two different pHs. In the end, a comparison of the biological analysis results showed that the CS with natural origin could improve the capability of the final system.

CS with medium molecular weight (MW 50000–190,000 Da, and degree of deacetylation 80.3%), 3–(4, 5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole dihydrochloride (DAPI), and CUR were products of Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. Citric acid (CA), sodium hydroxide, iron(III) nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3.9H2O), terephthalic acid (TPA), ethanol, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), glacial acetic acid, tween 80, n-hexane, formaldehyde (FA, 37 wt%), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Merck. The used materials in the biological tests were supplied from Gibco, USA.

Bruker Instruments (model Aquinox 55, Germany) were used to perform the FT-IR experiment of the samples in the 4000–400 cm−1 range as KBr pellets. XRD spectra were recorded at room temperature by using the Siemens diffractometer. The scanning was done in Cu-Kα radiation at 35 kV. Scanning electron microscopy and the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis were performed by using a TESCANMIRA3 scanning electron microscope operating at 5 kV. Zeta potential of the samples was measured by using the Horiba-SZ-100Z (Japan). Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–Vis) spectra were obtained by using the Shimadzu 1700 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were received by using a gas adsorption analyzer (BELsorp, Japan) at −196 °C. PerkinElmer (LS45) spectrofluorometer was applied for the recording of the PL spectra. DNA morphology of the cells was observed by a fluorescence microscope (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan).

GQDs were synthesized through the direct pyrolysis of CA [53]. In this way, CA (2 g) was poured into the container and the set was placed in a heating mantle with 200 °C. The pyrolysis of the CA was continued for 30 min at this temperature and the white powder of CA was converted to orange liquid. Under strong stirring, the achieved orange liquid was added dropwise into an aqueous solution of NaOH (100 mL, 10 mg/mL) and neutralization was continued to obtain a pH of 7. Thereafter, the obtained aqueous solution of GQDs was concentrated under the reduced pressure and finally was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MIL-88(Fe) in the presence of the GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was performed according to MIL-88(Fe) synthesis, the only difference was the synthesis of the MIL-88(Fe) in the presence of the GQDs [22]. Briefly, Fe(NO3)3. 9H2O (2.5 g) and TPA (1 g) were poured in 25 mL of DMF and the mixture was stirred for 5 min to completely dissolve. In the following, the mixture was sonicated for 5 min by using an ultrasonic bath to be uniform. The 50 mL of ethanol/water (1:1) was added to the reaction mixture and the mixture stirred and sonicated respectively for another 5 min and 30 min. GQDs were added to the MOF precursor and the mixture was stirred and sonicated for 30 min. In the next step, the mixture was moved to a round-bottom balloon and the temperature of the reaction was set at 85 °C. After 12 h, the produced GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was centrifuged and washed several times with ethanol and water. Finally, the obtained product was dried at 60 °C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading of CUR in GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para:
For the loading of CUR in GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), 100 mg of GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was dispersed in the CUR solution (500 ppm, ethanol). The mixture was stirred in the dark condition and room temperature. After 72 h, CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was separated by using the centrifuge and for the removing of the physically adsorbed CUR, CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) washed with a small amount of ethanol for two times. The drug loading tests were performed in triplicate. The amount of the loaded CUR was determined by using the UV–Vis spectroscopy at 424 nm. Eqs. (1) and (2) were used for calculation of the CUR loading capacity (%) and CUR entrapment efficiency (%).
(1)
Drug loading capacity
% w
=
Mass of
CUR
in carrier
Mass of carrier
× 100
(2)
Drug entrapment efficiency
% w
=
Mass of
CUR
in carrier
Mass of
CUR
fed
initially
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Facile coating of the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) with CS
ce:section.ce:para: For the coating of the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) with CS biopolymer, at first, the CS (0.5 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of the aqueous solution having 2% of glacial acetic acid. Then CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) (0.075 g) was added slowly to the CS solution and the mixture was dispersed by using the ultrasonic bath. The mixture of CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) and CS was added dropwise to the olive oil (50 mL). The 0.15 mL of Tween 80 was added to the mixture and the stirring of the mixture was continued for another 10 min to stabilize the formed W/O emulsion. In the following, FA (0.35 mL) was added drop by drop to the mixture, the mixture was stirred for 1 h at 50 °C and then for 4 h at room temperature. The produced microspheres washed with n-hexane and water and finally were dried at a vacuum oven for 24 h. The CS/CUR was prepared in the same way described above by adding the CUR solution in ethanol (3 mL, 1 mg/mL) instead of CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) into the CS solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study the swelling of CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) microspheres
ce:section.ce:para:
For the swelling study, the specific amount of the prepared CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) microspheres were weighed (Wi) and then immersed in 10 mL of swelling environments (pH 7.4 and pH 5.0). The microspheres were kept in the buffers for overnight at 37 °C to allow the microspheres to equilibrate. The swollen microspheres were removed from the buffer solution and the excess solution carefully was removed from the surface with a Whatman filter paper. After weighing the swollen microspheres, the Weq was determined. The swelling values were calculated by determining the equilibrium weight of microspheres and by using Eq. 3. The swelling test was repeated three times and the presented data are the average of the obtained results.
(3)
Swelling
%
=
Weq − Wi
Wi
x
100
In the Eq. (3), the Wi and Weq respectively are the initial and equilibrium weight of the microspheres.

The CUR release profiles of the samples were investigated in two different conditions (pH 7.4 and pH 5.0) that respectively simulated the pH of the normal and cancer cells condition. Briefly, 20 mg of the samples were dispersed in 10 mL of buffer solutions of pH 5.0 and pH 7.4 at 37 °C and were placed on the shaker for continuous shaking under the dark conditions. In the predetermined time intervals, 3 mL of the supernatant was sampled. For keeping the volume of the release medium constant, the 3 mL of the fresh buffer was readded. The amount of the released CUR was calculated through obtaining the absorbance of the sampled solution. By using the calibration curve and Eq. (4), the amount of the released CUR was determined.
(4)
Drug release
% w
=
Amount of released
CUR
Amount of loaded
CUR
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the CUR release kinetic
ce:section.ce:para:
To explain the CUR release mechanism, the in vitro drug release data were evaluated kinetically. In this regard, the achieved data of the CUR release were fitted to four common kinetic models; Zero-order (Eq. (5)), First-order (Eq. (6)), Higuchi (Eq. (7)), and Korsmeyer-Peppas (Eq. (8)) models. Korsmeyer-Peppas model is valid only in the 60% of the cumulated drug release curve. The first 60% of the release curve was fitted to the studied kinetic models [54,55]. The correlation coefficient (R2) in each case was obtained after fitting the pure data to each equation. The obtained value of ‘n’ diffusional exponent display the mechanism of drug release kinetics; n ≤ 0.45, 0.45<n > 1, and n = 1 respectively suggest that the drug release from the fabricated carrier via Fickian diffusion, non-Fickian transport, and a case-II transport.
(5)
Q =
k 0
t
(6)
LnQ = Ln
Q 0
−
k 1
t
(7)
Q =
k H
t
1 2
(8)
LnQ = nLnt + Ln
k p
where the Q, k0, k1, kH, and kp are the cumulative CUR releases percentage, rate constants of CUR release for Zero-order, First-order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas models, respectively.

MDA-MB 231 cells as a model of the breast cancer cell line were selected to determine the percentage of cancer cell-killing after treatment with CUR, CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). In this regard, MDA-MB 231 cells were cultured in 96-well plates, and then the cells incubated at 37 °C under the CO2 (5%) atmosphere. After 24 h, the cells were treated with samples in the concentration range of 6–96 μg/mL and incubated for 24 h. The solution in each well eliminated and then MTT solution (200 μL, 5 mg/mL) was replaced with them. The cells were incubated for another 4 h. In the following, after removing the unreacted MTT solution, the DMSO (200 μL) was added to the wells to dissolve the formed blue formazan crystals. ELISA plate reader was used to determine the solution absorbance at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630 nm. Additionally, the cytotoxicity of the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was investigated on normal breast cells (MCF 10A) as the above-mentioned procedure. The MTT assays were repeated three times and data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3).

A fluorescence microscope was used to observe the change in the morphology of the nucleus after 24 h treatment with CUR, CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). Briefly, MDA-MB 231 cells in six-well plates having coverslips were treated with samples in their half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50). After 24 h incubation, the cells were washed with PBS and fixed at room temperature by using the paraformaldehyde (3 wt%). For removing the extra of the paraformaldehyde, the cells were washed with PBS three times. In the following, the cells permeabilized with Triton X-100 (0.1% w/v) and finally washed with PBS and stained with DAPI. The DNA morphology of the cells was pictured by using a fluorescence microscope.

Designing and preparation of the drug delivery systems with a porous structure, more biocompatibility, and controlled drug release behavior is valuable in biomedicine. Taking this into account, porous MIL-88(Fe) was synthesized in the presence of GQDs to obtain the new porous nanohybrid with improved features in comparison with each of them and more functional groups in its structure that could be valuable for the drug delivery system. CUR as a natural hydrophobic drug was loaded in the prepared GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) nanohybrid. In continue, for achieving the drug carrier with more biocompatibility CUR/GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was coated with CS biopolymer as a natural, safe, and pH-responsive biological macromolecule. The schematic of the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) nanohybrid preparation, CUR loading, and encapsulation with CS is displayed in Scheme 1 .

FT-IR spectra of GQDs, GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), CS, and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) were obtained to characterize the structures of prepared samples. The recorded spectra are presented in Fig. 1 . In the FT-IR spectrum of the GQDs, the observed peaks at 3248 cm−1 and 3031 cm−1 respectively are assigned to the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl and aromatic -CH groups. In this spectrum, the symmetric stretching vibrations of -COO− and stretching vibration of CO appeared at 1394 cm−1 and 1122 cm−1 respectively. Additionally, the broad peaks in the 1600–1800 cm−1 of the GQDs spectrum could arise from the asymmetric stretching frequency of COO− and CC stretching vibration which merged with together [56]. After the synthesis of the MIL-88(Fe) in the presence of the GQDs, the new peaks appeared, as well as, the position some of the peaks changed which could be a reason for the in situ formation of the MIL-88(Fe) in the presence of the GQDs. In the spectrum the appeared peaks at 1596 cm−1 and 1392 cm−1 are assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate groups that coordinated to the Fe ions and the appearance of them could be a reason for the MIL-88(Fe) preparation. Moreover, the vibrations of -OH, CC, and CH appeared at 3392 cm−1, 1666 cm−1, and 683 cm−1 respectively. The CUR drug showed the main peaks at 3450 cm−1, 3058 cm−1, 1628 cm−1, 1509 cm−1, 1428 cm−1, 1278 cm−1, and 1024 cm−1 that are assigned to phenolic OH stretching vibration, stretching vibration of the -CH groups, stretching vibration of the CC of the benzene ring, CO stretching vibration, bending vibration of the olefinic CH, stretching vibration of the CO, and C-O-C. In the FTIR spectrum of the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), the main peaks representing both of the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) and CUR were observed. The lower intensity of the CUR peaks in comparison to the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) peaks could arise from the lower content of the CUR in CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). In the FT-IR spectrum, the CS showed the signature peaks at 3600–3300 cm−1 that it is related to the amine, and hydroxyl groups, as well as, intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonds. Additionally, the observed peaks at 2924 cm−1, 1640 cm−1, 1616 cm−1, 1300–1460 cm−1, and 1000–1200 cm−1 respectively are related to the stretching vibration of the -CH, CO of acetamide, bending vibration of the NH, stretching vibration of the CN, and stretching vibration of the CO [57]. Successful coating of the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) with CS as microsphere could verify via appearance the FT-IR peaks both of the CS and CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) in CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) FT-IR spectrum. Moreover, the appeared peak at 1586 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibration of the formed imine bond as a result of the crosslinking reaction [49].

The EDX analysis was performed to further verify the successful synthesis of MIL-88(Fe) in the presence of GQDs, as well as, the coating of the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) with CS polysaccharide. The obtained EDX pattern for the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) is displayed in Fig. 2a. The observed peak related to the Fe element arises from the formation of the MIL-88(Fe) and could approve the success in the synthesis of the MIL-88(Fe) in the presence of GQDs. Moreover, the EDX spectrum and elemental mapping in cross-section recorded for the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) to investigate the efficiency of the encapsulation method and how the distribution of the elements in the microsphere structure. The EDX mapping displayed the uniform dispersion of the elements and high content for Fe element, which could be attributed to the efficient coating of the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) with CS in the microsphere preparation step.

The porosity of the drug delivery systems based on the MOFs is a valuable advantage for theirs and it provides more sites for drug loading. By considering this, the Brunauer- Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis was run at 77 K for GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) and the obtained nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) plots are shown in Fig. 3a,b. According to the IUPAC classification, GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) exhibited a type IV hysteresis loop that is related to a mesoporous material. The specific surface area of the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) calculated about 6.1242 m2g−1. The decrease in the specific surface area of the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) compare to the neat MIL-88(Fe) [58] may be related to the trapping of the GQDs in the MOF cavity and it is in good accordance with the previously published paper [22]. In addition, the total pore volume and mean pore size were obtained about 0.018 cm3g−1 and 11.405 nm respectively via the BJH method. By considering the porous structure of the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) loading of CUR inside the MOFs cavities can be one of the impact factors for the observed value of drug loading. For further evaluation of the prepared samples features, the zeta potential analysis was performed to obtain the surface charge of them. Therefore, to investigate the success in the microsphere preparation steps the zeta potential of the GQDs, GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was measured and the obtained results are presented in Fig. 3c. GQDs showed the negative surface charge which results from the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in its structure. GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) obtained about 5.4 mV that exhibited the success in the in situ synthesis of MIL-88(Fe) with a positive charge. Finally, the coating with CS polysaccharide was confirmed via the shifting of the zeta potential to the higher positive charge (32.2 mV) which is related to the positive surface charge of the chitosan. All of the obtained data from the zeta potential are in good accordance with FT-IR results and approve the success in the drug carrier preparation steps.

XRD spectra of the samples were recorded in 2θ 5–70° to study the crystalline structure of the samples in the step by step of this work. As it is shown in Fig. 4a, for the synthesized GQDs the main XRD peak was observed at 2θ 24° that shows the formation of the GQDs through the pyrolysis of CA. The Bragg equation obtained the interlayer spacing of the synthesized GQDs with a hexagonal lattice structure about 3.70 Å that it is like to the gained value for the graphitic materials. After synthesis of the MIL-88(Fe) in the presence of the GQDs, the related peak to the GQDs was disappeared and only the three new peaks appeared at 2θ 9.13°, 17.46°, and 28.00° which they imply the growth of MIL-88(Fe) in the presence of GQDs. The disappearance of the GQDs crystalline peak in the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) spectrum is in agreement with the previously published results and it may outcomes from the formation of the interactions between the GQDs and MIL-88(Fe) during the synthesis of GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) [59]. CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) showed the one main broad peak at 2θ 15–25° of its XRD spectrum, which could be attributed to the formation of the amorph structure because of the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) capsulation with CS biopolymer. All of the obtained results from the XRD analysis could be good evidence for success in the preparation of the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) as a new drug delivery system. In the following, in other to investigate the intrinsic bioimaging potential of the prepared system, the PL spectra of the samples were studied and the obtained spectra are shown in Fig. 4b. In this regard, GQDs were excited by using a UV light at 360 nm. The synthesized GQDs displayed strong emission peaks at 460 nm. The PL intensity of GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) in the same excitation wavelength is lower than bare GQDs, as well as, they showed a partial shift in wavelength. Moreover, as it is visible from Fig. 4b, the PL spectra of GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) are wider than GQDs and a splitting pattern in their spectrum was detected. The observed changes in the PL spectra after modification reactions in comparison with neat GQDs could arise from the inner photoluminescence reabsorption, partial quenching, filter effect, and the presence of the non-photoluminescent portions in their structure. However, the PL intensity of the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) is appropriate for proposing the prepared system as a potential bioimaging vehicle [60].

The microscopic image of GQDs, GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) are depicted in Fig. 5 . SEM image of GQDs showed homogeneous spherical morphology with an average diameter of about 5 nm (Calculated by using the software program image J). SEM image of the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was recorded in two different magnification. The evaluation of the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) SEM images displays that the GQDs closely attached to the MIL-88(Fe). As shown in Fig. 5, SEM image of CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) exhibited a uniform distribution of microspheres which further demonstrates the formation of the green coat of CS on the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) nanohybrid as a microsphere.

Swelling is one of the effecting factors, which could influence the drug release behavior of each drug delivery system. In the current work, the swelling of the prepared CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) microspheres was investigated at two different buffer solutions, which simulated the pH of the cancer cells and physiological environments. The obtained results (Fig. 6a) show that the swelling percentage of microspheres in pH 5 is higher than pH 7.4. This observation could be referred that as the concentration of proton increases, the more amine groups of chitosan are protonated. As a result, the electrostatic repulsion between the charged groups of the microspheres leads to the structural relaxation and penetration of the buffers to the microsphere's structure. The observed pH-sensitive swelling behavior is valuable in drug delivery to the cancerous organs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug load and delivery studies
ce:section.ce:para: CUR was selected as a model of anticancer drugs with a natural origin, which used for treating a wide range of cancers. However, its poor aqueous solubility is its main drawback. Herein, it has been tried to overcome this limitation via nanoscience and obtain the system with good CUR loading and controlled release with enhanced bioavailability in the simulated physiological environment of cancer cells medium. CUR loading capacity and entrapment efficiency for the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) were obtained about 53.60% and 5.36% respectively and listed in Fig. 6b. This finding is evidenced via the FT-IR of CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) which displays the contribution of CUR in the pattern (Fig. 1). The loading of CUR on GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) results from the porosity and the presence of the special functional groups; hydroxyl, carboxyl, and aromatic ring in the structure of the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) and it can be explained by several phenomena; 1) Electrostatic interaction and H-bonding which occurs between the polar groups of the CUR and nanocarrier. 2) Capturing of the CUR in the MOF cavity. 3) π-π stacking interactions between the aromatic rings of CUR and GQDs.

For evaluation of the anticancer drug delivery ability of the samples the drug release experiments were performed in the simulated normal and cancer cell conditions. In vitro CUR release profile from the CS/CUR and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was determined by using the UV–Vis spectroscopy and the obtained drug release behavior and supposed schematic of CUR release are shown in Fig. 7 . The obtained results showed that the trend of the CUR releases for the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) is similar between two medium, however, a careful examination of the graphs shows that the curcumin releases is higher in pH 5.0 compared to the pH 7.4. The obtained higher bioavailability of the CUR in pH 5 than pH 7.4 is valuable in cancer treatment and proposed that the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) could be a hopeful candidate in cancer therapy. In the acidic solution, the remaining amine groups of CS will be protonated, therefore, generated repulsive force between the formed positive charges, leads to the more swell up and faster drug release. In contrast, in the medium with pH 7.4, the swelling value is lower, hence the diffusion of the buffer to the microspheres and drug release rate is lesser [48].The lower stability of the Schiff base and cleavage of the imine bonds (C=N) (formed in the crosslinking reaction) could be another reason for the higher drug release in acidic conditions [49,61]. Additionally in the acidic medium π–π stacking interaction between CUR and aromatic network weakens and drug releases increases. The primary comparison of CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) with CS/CUR exhibits a pH-sensitive and sustained release for both of them with a more sustained drug release profile within 72 h for CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). The interaction between the GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) nanohybrid and CUR could play a role in both load and release process and led to a longer path for the CUR migration from CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) into the release medium, as compared with CS/CUR. In general, the rate of drug release could depend on the interaction between the polymer molecules and drug, the interaction between nanohybrid and drug, swelling amount, and the solubility of the drug. According to the obtained drug release results CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) could be suggested to improve the CUR bioavailability for the absorption in the simulated medium with cancer cells condition [7,60,62,63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the CUR release kinetics from the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of the CUR releases from the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) microsphere was studied by fitting the obtained experimental data with four common release kinetic equations. The obtained plots and results respectively are displayed in Fig. S1, Fig. S2 and Table 1 . From the comparison of the obtained correlation coefficients (R2) for each release kinetic model, it is understood that in both release medium the release kinetics are best fitted with the First-order kinetic model. Further, the experimental model also revealed the non-Fickian behavior of drug release in both studied conditions.

To evaluate the cancer cell killing ability of the CUR, CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe), and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) the standard MTT assay was performed against MDA-MB 231 cells after incubation at different concentrations from 6 to 96 μg/mL. The extracted results from the MTT test showed that the increase in the treatment dosage leads to more cytotoxicity for the tested samples. After 24 h incubation, the MTT test obtained ~62% and ~50% of MDA-MB 231 cell viability for CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) respectively (Fig. 8a). The observed higher cell viability against MDA-MB 231 cell for CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) in comparison with CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) could be because of the sustained release of CUR into the cancer cells and presence the CS biopolymer in its structure of CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) [2,61]. Moreover, the cytocompatibility of the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) and CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was investigated against MCF 10A as a model of normal cells. According to Fig. 8b, up to 80% of cell viability is observed even after treatment in high concentrations. The slight increase in cell viability of CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) compared to the CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) revealed that coating with CS reduces the toxicity of the system. Generally, the MTT test results showed that the CS as a natural polysaccharide could act as a useful coat for improving the biocompatibility of drug delivery systems.

For more evaluation of the biocompatibility and MTT analysis results, the fluorescence imaging of the treated MDA-MB 231 cells was studied and the obtained images are displayed in Fig. 9 . The obtained images from the treated cells after the DAPI staining revealed that the number of cells in CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) treated cells is higher than CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). This observation displays the biocompatibility of the CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) as a drug delivery system. Moreover, the detected fragmented nuclei in treated cells with samples and free CUR indicates the apoptosis of cells due to the anti-cancer properties of CUR drug.

Briefly, the MIL-88(Fe) was prepared in the presence of the GQDs to achieve the porous nanovehicle with more functional groups and appropriate drug loading ability. The success in the synthesis was verified by using numerous techniques. CUR was loaded about 53.60% in the synthesized GQDs@MIL-88(Fe). In the following, to achieve the system with more biocompatibility the prepared CUR/GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) was coated with CS biopolymer. After 72 h, the total value of the released CUR at pH 5.0 was 39%, whereas, only 17% of CUR was released at pH 7.4. The prepared CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) microsphere presented the pH-sensitive swelling behavior and controlled release profile for the CUR which fitted the first-order kinetics model. In addition, the kinetics study revealed the non-Fickian behavior of drug release in both studied conditions. MTT and DAPI test results showed that the prepared CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) is safe with the capability of the cancer cells killing through the CUR releases. Increasing the biocompatibility of CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) in comparison with CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) probably related to its coating with biocompatible CS polysaccharide. The collectively obtained results suggested that CS/CUR@GQDs@MIL-88(Fe) could be a promising candidate as a cancer treatment biovehicle with pH-sensitive drug release behavior.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Malihe Pooresmaeil carried out the experiment and wrote the manuscript with support from Hassan Namazi. Hassan Namazi supervised the project. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (Grant Number 948645103) and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, the Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for their financial support of this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.06.183.
